narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which flags you as a higher-risk admit compared to others. This prediction draws from your academic record, like being in the second decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's fifth or sixth decile position. Your LSAT score of 25 and undergraduate GPA of 2.4 also sit well below the averages of 36.7 and 3.21 seen across applicants. As a male Asian student from a middle-income family planning full-time studies, your profile aligns with some common traits, but your performance metrics stood out as the main drivers. The model's decision was most swayed by your third-year decile rank, which had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, followed closely by your first-year decile, then your LSAT score. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender followed in that order of influence, each nudging the prediction slightly more toward risk. None of these factors pulled in the opposite direction for you; instead, they collectively highlighted concerns relative to stronger-performing peers. In contrast, typical admitted students boast higher deciles, LSATs above 35, and GPAs over 3.2, tipping their predictions firmly toward passing the bar. While demographics like gender, race, income, and study status are harder to shift, you can realistically retake the LSAT to boost that score—many students improve by 5-10 points with focused prep. Strengthening your recent academic deciles through grad-level courses or post-bac work could also help rebuild your profile. Consider targeted bar prep programs early, as they address the root predictors of success. With these steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle and flip the model's outlook in your favor. Hang in there—many overcome similar hurdles and thrive.
